coredata.dc:title: Colorimetric sensor and visual enzyme sheets for sensitive detection of dimethoate residue in vegetables based on laccase-like activity of coral-like silver citrate
coredata.dc:creator: Xia, Lian
Han, Jianxun
Huang, Xiaohuan
Niu, Xiaojuan
Lin, Xin
Wu, Yuangen
coredata.dc:description: The overutilization of dimethoate may lead to its deposition in both the environment and crops, which potentially induces neurological, respiratory, and hepatic abnormalities upon absorption by organisms. Colorimetry can accurately determine dimethoate, but there are still many challenges in the rapid, convenient, and point-of-care detection. Herein, based on the inhibitory effect of dimethoate on the laccase activity of silver citrate (AgCit), a smartphone-assisted colorimetric sensor and rapid detection enzyme sheets were constructed. It was demonstrated that dimethoate could adsorb on the surface of AgCit and inhibit the production of semiquinone radicals in the reaction system. Then, the AgCit laccase activity was inhibited, causing the colorimetry sensor and the enzyme sheets turned light red or even colorless. The constructed sensor has higher sensitivity and the limit of detection for dimethoate is 1.0 μg⋅L−1. Moreover, the developed enzyme sheets have good selectivity and specificity, and display the detection results within 5 min. Thus, the colorimetric sensor and the constructed enzyme sheets are expected to be used for large-scale preliminary detection of dimethoate residues in actual agricultural products.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Food analysis
Organophosphorus pesticide
Mimetics
Colorimetric detection
Paper-based assay
Smartphone-assisted sensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation and identification of coral-like AgCit
Construction of the colorimetric sensor and enzyme sheets
Analysis of dimethoate in actual samples
Selection of carrier membrane of the enzyme sheets
Detection of dimethoate in actual samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 32360621 , 32160603 , 31760486 ), the Guizhou Provincial Basic Research Program (ZK[2021]Key Project 037), the Guizhou Provincial Key Technology R&D Program (QKHZC[2023]123), the Guizhou Provincial Outstanding Young Science and Technology Talent Project ([2021]5628), and the General Project for Technological Innovation and Development in the Field of Social Development in Guiyang City .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) SEM image of the prepared AgCit. (B) XRD patterns of the coral-like AgCit.

(A) Schematic illustration of the colorimetric sensor for dimethoate detection. (B) Feasibility of the colorimetric sensor for dimethoate detection. Sample 1: HEPES + AgCit + 2,4-DP + 4-AAP; Sample 2: HEPES + AgCit + 2,4-DP + 4-AAP + 20 μg⋅L−1 dimethoate; Sample 3: HEPES + AgCit + 2,4-DP + 4-AAP + 50 μg⋅L−1 dimethoate; Sample 4: HEPES + AgCit + 2,4-DP + 4-AAP + 100 μg⋅L−1 dimethoate; Sample 5: HEPES + AgCit + 4-AAP; Sample 6: HEPES + AgCit + 4-AAP+ 50 μg⋅L−1 dimethoate. (C) Schematic demonstration of the visual enzyme sheets for dimethoate detection.

(A) Feasibility of the visual enzyme sheets for dimethoate detection. Sample 1: HEPES; Sample 2: HEPES + AgCit; Sample 3: HEPES + 2,4-DP + 4-AAP; Sample 4: HEPES + AgCit + 2,4-DP + 4-AAP; Sample 5: HEPES + AgCit + 2,4-DP + 4-AAP + 0.2 mg⋅L−1 dimethoate; Sample 6: HEPES + AgCit + 2,4-DP + 4-AAP + 0.5 mg⋅L−1 dimethoate; (B) Changes of semiquinone radical in different solutions. Sample 1: AgCit + 2,4-DP; sample 2: AgCit + dimethoate + 2,4-DP. (C) Plots of reaction rate versus the AgCit quantities with dimethoate content. (D) Lineweaver−Burk plots of 1/V versus 1/2,4-DP.

(A) Visual images and absorbance change (ΔA) of reaction solution with dimethoate content. Inset: absorbance change (ΔA) curve of reaction solution at low content of dimethoate. Linear equation: y = 0.017×−0.20, R2 = 0.988. (B) Images and gray variation of dimethoate with different quantities were detected by the enzyme sheets. Linear equation: y = 0.017 + 0.94×, R2 = 0.992. (C) The color images and absorbance value (ΔA) of colorimetric sensor with different competitive pesticides (50 μg⋅L−1). Sample 1: AgCit; Sample 2: AgCit + DMT; Sample 3: AgCit + OMF; Sample 4: AgCit + MLT; Sample 5: AgCit + MP; Sample 6: AgCit + ICP; Sample 7: AgCit + LCT; Sample 8: AgCit + CBF; Sample 9: AgCit + CBL; Sample 10: AgCit + IMI. (D) Images and gray variation of different targets (0.6 mg⋅L−1) were detected by enzyme sheets.
Detection of dimethoate with the constructed methods and GC in actual spiked vegetable samples.

head.ce:title: Colorimetric sensor and visual enzyme sheets for sensitive detection of dimethoate residue in vegetables based on laccase-like activity of coral-like silver citrate

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Plant Resource Conservation and Germplasm Innovation in Mountainous Region (Ministry of Education), College of Life Sciences/Institute of Agro-bioengineering, Guizhou University, Guiyang, 550025, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Plant Resource Conservation and Germplasm Innovation in Mountainous Region (Ministry of Education)
College of Life Sciences/Institute of Agro-bioengineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Plant Resource Conservation and Germplasm Innovation in Mountainous Region (Ministry of Education), College of Life Sciences/Institute of Agro-bioengineering, Guizhou University, Guiyang 550025, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Guizhou Pony Test Technology Co., LTD, Guiyang, 550027, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guizhou Pony Test Technology Co., LTD

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Guizhou Pony Test Technology Co., LTD, Guiyang 550027, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Comprehensive Technology Center of Guiyang Customs District, Guiyang, 550081, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Comprehensive Technology Center of Guiyang Customs District

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Comprehensive Technology Center of Guiyang Customs District, Guiyang 550081, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. College of Life Sciences, Guizhou University, Guiyang, 550025, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The overutilization of dimethoate may lead to its deposition in both the environment and crops, which potentially induces neurological, respiratory, and hepatic abnormalities upon absorption by organisms. Colorimetry can accurately determine dimethoate, but there are still many challenges in the rapid, convenient, and point-of-care detection. Herein, based on the inhibitory effect of dimethoate on the laccase activity of silver citrate (AgCit), a smartphone-assisted colorimetric sensor and rapid detection enzyme sheets were constructed. It was demonstrated that dimethoate could adsorb on the surface of AgCit and inhibit the production of semiquinone radicals in the reaction system. Then, the AgCit laccase activity was inhibited, causing the colorimetry sensor and the enzyme sheets turned light red or even colorless. The constructed sensor has higher sensitivity and the limit of detection for dimethoate is 1.0 μg⋅L−1. Moreover, the developed enzyme sheets have good selectivity and specificity, and display the detection results within 5 min. Thus, the colorimetric sensor and the constructed enzyme sheets are expected to be used for large-scale preliminary detection of dimethoate residues in actual agricultural products.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Dimethoate (DMT) is a highly effective and broad-spectrum acaricide widely used in cash crops and production (Ahmad, et al., 2022; Hyun, Lee, Lee, & Choi, 2021). Excessive use of dimethoate will result in the accumulation of its residues in water, soil and plant tissues (Anicijevic, Petkovic, Pasti, & Lazarevic-Pasti, 2022; Jocic et al., 2022). More worrisome is that the contact of dimethoate with humans leads to a large accumulation of acetylcholine (Lu, Wang, Zhan, & Wu, 2020). It will make the central nervous system and cholinergic nerve overexcited, and finally cause abnormalities in the nervous system, reproductive system and liver (Xu, He, Xie, & He, 1997). Due to the wide range of pesticide residues in vegetables and fruits, the Chinese Ministry of Agriculture and Rural Affairs (GB2763-2021) stipulates that the maximum residue level of dimethoate in part of vegetables is 500 μg⋅kg−1. Thus, it is urgent to construct a rapid, convenient and sensitive method for dimethoate residue detection. Presently, the detection of dimethoate at home and abroad mainly relies on large instruments such as gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (GC-MS) and gas chromatography (GC) (Alves, et al., 2012; Bempelou et al., 2023; Jiang et al., 2015). Although such instrumental methods can sensitively determine the lower concentration of dimethoate, there are still some shortcomings like expensive instruments, high requirements for operators and high maintenance costs (Qi, Wu, Kang, Hu, & Yu, 2020). Lately, researchers have gradually established various methods for dimethoate detection, such as enzyme-linked immunoassay kits, electrochemical and colorimetric methods (Borah, Dutta, Gogoi, Medhi, & Puzari, 2017; F. Li et al., 2022; J.-l. Wang, Xia, Zhang, Hu, & Lin, 2012). However, the existing methods for dimethoate detection have significant drawbacks. For example, easy inactivation of antibodies, susceptibility to interference with enzyme inhibition methods, and reliance on specialized equipment for electrochemistry (Bedair, et al., 2022). Moreover, colorimetric methods are mostly based on the aggregation effect of the nanozymes themselves or the activity of oxidase and peroxidase, which are easily affected by other conditions and may lead to inaccurate detection results (J. Li, Zuo, Liu, Xie, & Huang, 2021). Currently, it is urgent to develop new materials or simulate enzyme activity to construct convenient and sensitive new methods for dimethoate detection, which is of great importance for online monitoring of dimethoate residues in food and preventing pesticide poisoning.

Laccase mimetics have become one of the researches focuses as green catalysts in recent years (L. Huang et al., 2023; Niu et al., 2023). Laccase is a class of natural copper-containing polyphenol oxidases that are mostly applied to degrade phenolic pollutants (Janusz, et al., 2020; Lei, Yang, Song, Huang, & Li, 2022). However, natural laccase is weak in catalytic activity, difficult to extract and less stable, so researchers have worked to develop laccase mimics (Khatami, et al., 2022; Lin, Wang, Yu, Zhang, & Lu, 2022). In 2015, copper-containing carbon dots (Cu-CDs) with laccase mimetic enzymatic activity was first reported by Meng et al. (Ren, Liu, Ren, Tang, & Meng, 2015). Cu-CDs was able to effectively oxidize the phenolic compounds p-phenylenediamine and hydroquinone, which became the beginning of laccase mimetics research (Ren, et al., 2015). The reported laccase mimics are mainly applied for the detection and analysis of phenolic compounds like bisphenol, chlorophenol, dopamine and epinephrine (H. Huang et al., 2021; Zhu, Cui, Wen, Zhang, & Wang, 2022). For instance, researchers have synthesized the metal-organic framework material Cu/GMP for lignin degradation using guanine nucleotide (GMP) as the ligand and copper ions as the active center (Yang, et al., 2022). Coral-like silver citrate (AgCit) was first discovered which had laccase mimicking activity by Lee et al., and was used for the detection of small molecules like phenolics and epinephrine (Koyappayil, Kim, & Lee, 2021). Compared with natural laccase, laccase mimics exhibit stable catalysis at high pH, extreme temperature and high salt conditions with excellent kinetic parameters (Gao, Xiong, Jia, Li, & Li, 2022). Moreover, laccase catalysis requires only oxygen and no H2O2 participation, which makes the laccase catalysis assay results more reliable (Jeon, Baldrian, Murugesan, & Chang, 2012). Recently, our group has also successively utilized silver phosphate laccase activity and Ag2O to construct a colorimetric sensor for zearalenone and kanamycin (L. Huang et al., 2023; Niu et al., 2023). Unfortunately, there is no report on the dimethoate pesticide detection by laccase colorimetric sensors or enzyme sheets. Thus, the application of laccase simulation catalytic activity to pesticide residue detection in food is of great significance.

Herein, a significant inhibitory effect of dimethoate on the laccase-like activity of coral-like AgCit was discovered, and a colorimetric sensor was further constructed. The substrate 2,4-dichlorophenol could be catalyzed by the AgCit to form quinones, which subsequently combined with 4-aminoantipyrine to generate a red product with a characteristic absorption peak at 510 nm. Notably, the dimethoate can adsorb on the surface of the AgCit, mask the active site of AgCit and further inhibit its laccase-like activity. The color of the sensor then changes from red to light pink or even colorless, and the absorbance change value at 510 nm is linear. Subsequently, the AgCit was further immobilized on the carrier membrane as a chromogenic signal to prepare a visual rapid detection enzyme sheet. The constructed colorimetric sensor and enzyme sheets based on laccase-like activity provide a new scheme for pesticide detection, and broaden the application of laccase-like activity for the other substances detection.

AgNO3 powder, trisodium citrate, 4-hydroxyethyl piperazine ethane sulfonic acid (HEPES), 2,4-dichlorophenol (2,4-DP), 4-aminoantipyrine (4-AAP), methanol, sodium hydroxide (NaOH), N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF), dimethoate (DMT), omethoate (OMT), malathion (MLT), methyl parathion (MP), chlorpyrifos (ICP), lambda-cyhalothrin (LCT), carbofuran (CBF), carbaryl (CBL), imidacloprid (IMI) were purchased from Aladdin Corporation (Shanghai, China). Glass cellulose membrane (Ahlstrom 8964), polyester membranes (DL42, VL78 and VL98) and absorbed pad (H-1) and PVC based pad (DB-6) were obtained from Shanghai Jining Biotechnology Co. The 2,4-DP was dissolved with DMF, and other reagents were dissolved or diluted with ultrapure water. All reagents were at least of analytical purity grade and did not require further purification or treatment, and the experimental water was ultrapure.

The morphological characteristics of the materials were observed by scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Hitachi). The X-ray diffraction (XRD) (Rigaku Ultima diffractometer) was used to analyze the purity of the crystals. Electron spin resonance energy spectrometer (ESR) signal data were collected by an electron spin resonance energy spectrometer (BRUKE EMXPLUS). A constant temperature magnetic stirrer was applied to prepare AgCit material. Ultrapure water used for the experiments was prepared with a Millipore ultrapure water machine. The Microplate Spectrophotometer Varioskan Flash (Thermo Scientific, USA) was employed to collect UV/Vis spectra and absorbance values.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and identification of coral-like AgCit
ce:section.ce:para: The coral-like AgCit was prepared according to the reports (Koyappayil, et al., 2021). In brief, the AgNO3 powder and trisodium citrate were mixed slowly at a molar ratio of 3:1 for 30 min with a magnetic stirrer. The suspension was further left overnight and the precipitate was collected at 8000 rpm for 10 min. Then, the white precipitate was repeatedly rinsed three times with ultrapure water and dried at 80 °C. The obtained AgCit powder was placed in a light-proof drying vessel for storage. The materials were subsequently characterized by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) for surface morphology. X-ray diffraction (XRD) was employed to analyze the formation of AgCit and to determine whether the prepared AgCit is consistent with literature reports.

To investigate the binding mechanism of dimethoate and AgCit, the dimethoate (10 μL) and AgCit (50 μL) were added to a centrifuge tube, mixed well, and incubated for 10 min at 37 °C. Subsequently, the HEPES buffer (420 μL) and 2,4-DP solution (10 μL) were added to the above mixture. In the control group, 10 μL ultrapure water was used in place of the dimethoate solution. The ESR detector was employed to assess the presence of semiquinone free radicals in the reaction system, thereby determining the impact of dimethoate on the generation of 2,4-DP semiquinone free radicals catalyzed by the AgCit.

To explore the impact of dimethoate on the catalytic kinetics of AgCit, a mixture consisting of 10 μL dimethoate and 50 μL AgCit (1 mg⋅mL-1) was introduced into a centrifuge tube. The mixture was thoroughly mixed and subsequently incubated for 10 min at 37 °C. Then, 420 μL HEPES buffer, 10 μL 2,4-DP, and 10 μL 4-AAP solution were added to the mentioned mixture. Subsequently, the absorbance value of the reaction solution at a wavelength of 510 nm was measured for 100 s at intervals of 10 s, with the resulting data being recorded. According to Lambert-Beer law (A/l = αC), the number of products generated per unit of time is calculated, and the initial speed of the enzymatic reaction is further obtained. In the above formula, A and l are absorbance value and optical path (0.56 cm), respectively. The extinction coefficient (α) of the product is 13,600 M-1 cm-1 (Y. Wang, He, Li, Zhang, & Fu, 2017), while C is the concentration per unit time. Finally, the typical double reciprocal curve of enzymatic reaction was obtained, and the Km and Vmax values were further calculated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of the colorimetric sensor and enzyme sheets

ce:section.ce:para: A colorimetric sensor was constructed based on the change of laccase-like activity of AgCit (H. Li, Zhao, Wang, & Li, 2023). The procedure was as follows: 10 μL of dimethoate and 50 μL of AgCit (1 mg⋅mL−1) were added to a centrifuge tube, mixed well and incubated at 37 °C for 10 min. The HEPES buffer (420 μL), 2,4-DP (10 μL) and 4-AAP solution (10 μL) were added to the above mixture and mixed well to establish a colorimetric sensor for the dimethoate detection.

A physical adsorption method was used to immobilize coral-like AgCit material to prepare rapid detection enzyme sheets (D. Luo, Huang, Liu, Zou, & Wu, 2021). The carrier membrane was first cut into 1.5 × 1.5 cm2 and then placed in the HEPES buffer for 1 h and dried at 37 °C. The 10 μL AgCit solution dissolved in methanol was further added dropwise to the carrier membrane and dried at 37 °C for 30 min to develop an AgCit laccase sheet. Subsequently, the rapid detection enzyme sheet was assembled, and its structure mainly consisted of the based pad, absorbed pad and AgCit laccase sheet. The absorbed pad was firstly attached to the base pad, and then the AgCit laccase sheet was mounted on the above-mentioned based pad to assemble the rapid detection enzyme sheets.

Considering the effects of AgCit quantities, substrate content, buffer concentration, pH and reaction temperature on the detection accuracy and sensitivity of the colorimetric sensor, the above detection conditions were carefully optimized. To validate the color development accuracy and intensity of the enzyme sheets, four carrier membrane materials, such as the glass cellulose membrane and different polyester membranes were selected to prepare the rapid detection enzyme sheets. The carrier membranes were pretreated (D. Luo et al., 2021), and the AgCit was further immobilized on them. When the enzyme sheets were assembled, the substrate with the same content was further added dropwise to the sample pad, and the color development results were observed and recorded separately by the camera. According to the color development results, the type of carrier membrane for the rapid detection enzyme sheets was finally determined.

In a typical colorimetry process, 50 μL AgCit solution, 10 μL 2,4-DP and 10 μL dimethoate solution were added to a centrifuge tube, and mixed thoroughly. Then, 420 μL HEPES buffer and 10 μL 4-AAP solution were added to the above tube. In the control group, the dimethoate solution was replaced with the ultrapure water. The absorbance values of test samples (A1) and blank samples (A0) at 510 nm were recorded. Finally, the change of absorbance value (ΔA = A0-A1) is selected as the colorimetric signal to judge the response degree of the established colorimetric sensor to dimethoate.

For the dimethoate detection using the designed enzyme sheets, 10 μL of HEPES buffer (pH 7.4) and dimethoate solutions with various contents were dropped into the sample pad of the enzyme sheets and reacted for a while. Subsequently, 10 μL 2,4-DP and 10 μL 4-AAP substrates were added, and the color change was observed and recorded with the camera. The color intensity values of the negative control area (G0) and sample area (G) were further obtained by Image J, and then the colorimetric value ΔG (G0−G)/G0 was calculated to determine the dimethoate concentration and perform qualitative and quantitative analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of dimethoate in actual samples
ce:section.ce:para: Considering the widespread residues of dimethoate in agricultural products (S. Li et al., 2015), vegetables like cowpea, cabbage and pepper were chosen as actual samples to validate the practicality of the constructed colorimetric sensor and enzyme sheets. The actual sample pretreatment method was performed according to the reference to the previous literature (F. Luo et al., 2023). In brief, the cowpea, cabbage and pepper were weighed and evenly sprayed with different concentrations of dimethoate solution. The actual samples were repeatedly eluted with 5 mL HEPES buffer (pH 7.4), and the eluted solutions were collected. Finally, the dimethoate content in the eluate was measured.

The morphology of the prepared AgCit was observed by scanning electron microscopy (SEM). As presented in Fig. 1 A, the prepared AgCit showed a coral-like shape, which is consistent with previous reports (J. Li et al., 2021). Moreover, X-ray diffraction (XRD) was applied to analyze the formation of the AgCit. As shown in Fig. 1B, the XRD spectra matching JCPDS card No. 00-001-0030 confirmed the formation of the AgCit, and there are no diffraction peaks of other impurities. In summary, the pure coral-like AgCit particulate material has been successfully prepared.

It was found that the laccase-like activity of the coral-like AgCit could be significantly inhibited by dimethoate. Herein, a colorimetric sensor for dimethoate detection was constructed, and the schematic is shown in Fig. 2 A. Without dimethoate, the AgCit combines with the substrate 2,4-DP to form a complex that causes 2,4-DP to lose electrons and produce semiquinone free radicals. The quinone formed is further combined with 4-AAP to form a red product with a characteristic absorption peak at 510 nm. Thus, the reaction solution is significantly red. In the presence of dimethoate, the active site of AgCit can be masked through the dimethoate adsorbed on the surface of coral-like AgCit. Then, the amount generation of semiquinone free radicals in the reaction system will decrease, and the laccase-like activity of coral-like AgCit can be finally inhibited. The reaction solution changes from red to light pink or even colorless, and the absorbance change value at 510 nm is linear with the dimethoate content.

To explore whether the established colorimetric sensor can effectively detect dimethoate, the color changes of different reaction solutions and the absorption spectra in the range of 400–750 nm were recorded (Fig. 2B). The pure coral-like AgCit could oxidize 2,4-DP to form quinone and reacted with 4-AAP to produce red complex with a characteristic peak of the absorption at 510 nm (Sample 1). Once dimethoate was added, the absorption peak of the sample instead gradually decreased with the increase of dimethoate quantities, and the color changed from red to light red or even colorless (Sample 2, Sample 3, and Sample 4). Interestingly, the AgCit could directly oxidize 4-AAP to produce a yellow product (Sample 5) when pure 4-AAP was present in the reaction solution. The color of the solution did not change when dimethoate was added (Sample 6). The above results indicate that dimethoate can significantly inhibit the laccase-like activity of AgCit and the degree of color change was positively correlated with the dimethoate quantities. Thus, the colorimetric sensor can be applied for rapid colorimetric detection of dimethoate residues in agricultural products. As can be seen from Fig. S1, the mixture of 2,4-DP and 4-AAP was colorless without AgCit (Sample S1). The color did not change significantly (Sample S2) when the dimethoate was added to the above mixture, and there was no characteristic absorption peak at 510 nm. In addition, the pure AgCit, 2,4-DP, or 4-AAP was added to the dimethoate solution without characteristic absorption peaks in the range of 400–700 nm (Sample S3–S8). The above results prove that the dimethoate only interacts with the AgCit, and other substances like 2,4-DP and 4-AAP do not interfere with the detection results.

Herein, the coral-like AgCit was selected as a chromogenic substance and immobilized on the carrier membrane. The visual rapid detection enzyme sheets for dimethoate detection were first designed, which consist of a based pad, absorbed pad and AgCit sheet (Fig. S2). The absorbed pad can receive the excess sample solution, concentrating the entire reaction area and facilitating observation of the reaction phenomenon. The principle of the rapid detection enzyme sheets is displayed in Fig. 2C. In the presence of the dimethoate, the AgCit immobilized in the rapid detection enzyme sheets would bind to the dimethoate, and only a small amount of 2,4-DP was catalyzed to form quinone. Thus, the rapid detection enzyme sheets appeared light red or even colorless after binding to 4-AAP. In the absence of the dimethoate, the AgCit immobilized in the enzyme sheets catalyzed 2,4-DP to form an amount of quinones and bound with 4-AAP. In this case, a large amount of red product is formed and the enzyme sheets appear dark red.

The different enzyme sheets were assembled to detect different sample solutions, and the detection data were gathered to validate whether the enzyme sheets could effectively detect dimethoate (Fig. 3 A). To observe the background signal of the carrier membrane with AgCit and its color development effect, HEPES buffer was dropped into Sample 1 (with AgCit) and Sample 2 (without AgCit), respectively. As shown in Fig. 3A, the sample pad in both Sample 1 and Sample 2 were not colored, indicating that almost no background signal was generated. The carrier membrane in Sample 3 was not immobilized with AgCit, and only 2,4-DP and 4-AAP solutions were added dropwise to the sample pad. The results showed that the pure 2,4-DP and 4-AAP solutions also did not produce a background signal that interfered with the assay results. Fortunately, the AgCit fully demonstrated its laccase activity in Sample 4, proving that AgCit could still catalyze 2,4-DP to produce quinone in paper-based materials, and then combine with 4-AAP to produce a red substance. The different concentrations of dimethoate were further added to the sample pad, it showed a significant red color (Sample 5–6) after 5 min. The change in grayscale value was positively correlated with the dimethoate quantities. These results indicate that dimethoate can interact with AgCit, which makes AgCit sites occupied. Thereby, 2,4-DP is difficult to be oxidized by AgCit into quinone that reacts with 4-AAP, resulting in a light red or even colorless appearance after dropping dimethoate. Therefore, the developed enzyme sheets can be quick and effective for dimethoate detection, and the results can be distinguished by the naked eye.

It has been reported in the literature that 2,4-DP can produce semiquinone radicals during its oxidation catalyzed by laccase (Y. Huang, Ren, & Qu, 2019; Koyappayil et al., 2021), which can be determined by ESR spectroscopy (Barbehenn, Poopat, & Spencer, 2003; Yuasa, Yamada, & Fukuzumi, 2007). To investigate the change of semiquinone radical in the AgCit catalyzed system, the ESR spectra of the reaction solution before and after adding dimethoate were measured. As presented in Fig. 3B, an obvious ESR signal appeared in the AgCit catalytic system, which indicated that AgCit could oxidize 2,4-DP to produce a large number of semiquinone radicals. Once the dimethoate solution was added, the ESR spectral signal of the catalytic system significantly decreased. The above results confirm that dimethoate could inhibit the production of semiquinone radicals in the catalytic system.

To reveal the inhibitory mechanism of dimethoate on coral-like AgCit, different concentrations of dimethoate were added to the AgCit solution and the catalytic rate was measured. The results are demonstrated in Fig. 3C, where three straight lines intersected at a point, and the slope of the straight lines decreased gradually with the increase of the dimethoate quantities. It indicates that the inhibitory effect of dimethoate on AgCit was a typical reversible inhibition and non-competitive reversible inhibition. The double inverse curve of the enzymatic reaction rate versus and substrate concentration was measured by varying the amount of dimethoate and 2,4-DP (Fig. 3D). The extension lines of the double inverse curves intersected at the same point on the abscissa, and the slope of the line rose with the dimethoate content. The K m value of the enzymatic reaction without dimethoate was calculated as 0.25 mM. Even in the presence of dimethoate solutions with varying concentrations (40 μg⋅L−1 and 80 μg⋅L−1), the K m value of the enzymatic reaction was still close to 0.25 mM. However, the V max value was calculated as 2 × 10−7 Ms−1 before the addition of dimethoate and gradually decreased to 1.49 × 10−7 Ms−1 or even 1.03 × 10−7 Ms−1 with the dimethoate. The above results elucidate that dimethoate does not alter the affinity of coralloidal AgCit for 2,4-DP, but affects the rate of AgCit catalysis for 2,4-DP.

To investigate the detection performance of the constructed colorimetric sensor under different buffer conditions, the pH and content of the HEPES buffer solution were optimized. The variation trend of absorbance at different pH was shown in Fig. S3A. The absorbance difference (ΔA) at 510 nm gradually increased as the pH rose, and reached the maximum value at pH 8.5. Once the pH exceeded 9.0, AgCit laccase activity diminished and ΔA gradually decreased. This may be attributed to excessive pH affecting the binding of AgCit to the substrate, leading to a weakening of the sensing signal. The above results expose that AgCit has good catalytic activity under neutral conditions, which provides a new idea for other laccase materials to construct colorimetric sensors under such neutral conditions. Subsequently, the impact of buffer concentration on the detection performance was further explored. As the buffer concentration increased, the ΔA of the reaction solution at 510 nm gradually increased, until the sensing signal reached its maximum value when the buffer content was closed to 20 mM (Fig. S3B). Once the buffer concentration was further increased, the sensing signal instead decreased gradually. The reason may be that high salt ion content was unfavorable for AgCit binding to 2,4-DP. Thus, the 20 mM of HEPES buffer (pH 8.5) was chosen for the subsequent experiments.

The enzyme content is extremely important for the construction of sensor and detection sensitivity. Thus, the AgCit content was investigated in detail (Fig. S4). As the AgCit quantities increased, the ΔA of the solution at 510 nm also gradually increased. Unexpectedly, when the AgCit concentration exceeded 0.1 mg⋅mL−1, the ΔA no longer changed significantly. Thus, 0.1 mg⋅mL−1 of AgCit is used as the optimal content for subsequent experiments. It is speculated that the combination of AgCit and 2,4-DP reaches a saturation state, and the absorbance value of the solution at 510 nm does not change significantly after increasing the AgCit quantities.

Substrate concentration is a key parameter in the detection accuracy of the colorimetric sensor, which is also related to the detection sensitivity of the sensor. The effects of 2,4-DP and 4-AAP quantities on dimethoate colorimetric sensors were explored. As shown in Fig. S5A, ΔA at 510 nm gradually increased as the 2,4-DP content rose, and ΔA no longer larger significantly when the content of 2,4-DP exceeded 0.1 mg⋅mL−1. Fig. S5B demonstrated the trend of absorbance at different quantities of the 4-AAP. As the 4-AAP content increased, the ΔA gradually rose and reached a maximum value at 0.03 mg⋅mL−1. However, ΔA instead gradually decreased when the 4-AAP concentration was higher than 0.03 mg⋅mL−1. The reason for this phenomenon may be that the enriched 4-AAP will bind to AgCit, occupying part of the binding site between AgCit and dimethoate, hindering the inhibitory effect of dimethoate on AgCit.

To investigate the effect of reaction temperature on the detection performance of the constructed colorimetric sensor, the dimethoate solution was added to AgCit and placed at different temperatures for 10 min. The absorbance of the solution was further measured after the buffer and substrate were added (Fig. S6). The reaction solution had the largest ΔA near room temperature, which indicated that room temperature would be beneficial for the interaction of dimethoate with AgCit. However, AgCit enzyme activity decreased sharply at 80 °C, which may be close to the decomposition of coral-like AgCit into metal silver when it exceeded 75 °C (Koyappayil, et al., 2021). In summary, dimethoate has a good inhibition effect on AgCit at room temperature, which enhances the application of dimethoate colorimetric sensor in field point-of-care detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selection of carrier membrane of the enzyme sheets
ce:section.ce:para: To explore the color effect of different carrier membranes in the rapid detection enzyme sheets, four carrier membranes were selected to immobilize AgCit and prepare the enzyme sheets. All enzyme sheets were added with 10 μL 2,4-DP solution (5 mg⋅mL−1) and 10 μL 4-AAP solution (1 mg⋅mL−1) for 5 min. The results presented in Fig. S7, Sample 1–4 were the color rendering effects of the enzyme sheets prepared by glass cellulose membrane and three different polyester membranes. Sample 1 clearly showed the characteristic red color catalyzed by laccase, and the color development area was concentrated and uniform. Sample 2 and Sample 4 respectively presented a deep purple that was inconsistent with the characteristic color red of laccase catalysis, and only Sample 3 showed yellow with scattered color regions. These results prove that the AgCit can be immobilized on the glass cellulose membrane (Ahlstrom 8964), and the laccase-like activity of AgCit was maintained in the carrier membrane. Moreover, the enzyme sheets showed good catalytic activity, and the color development was uniform and concentrated, which can meet the detection requirements.

To determine the sensitivity of the colorimetric sensor, different concentrations (20–200 μg⋅L−1) of dimethoate standard solutions were added to the solution and the corresponding absorbance values were recorded and analyzed. As can be seen from Fig. 4 A, the reaction solution was initially dark red, and the color of the reaction system gradually changed from dark red to light red with the increase of the dimethoate quantities. The reaction solution tended to be colorless after the dimethoate concentration was higher than 200 μg⋅L−1, and ΔA no longer changed significantly. The ΔA had a good linear relationship with the dimethoate concentration when the dimethoate content was in the range of 20–80 μg⋅L−1. The linear regression equation is y = 0.017×−0.20, R2 = 0.988. The limit of detection (LOD) for dimethoate was calculated as 1.0 μg⋅L−1 by 3σ/slope (Tang, et al., 2021), where σ is the standard deviation (n = 20) determined in the blank group measurement values, and the slope is the slope of the linear regression equation. The limit of quantification (LOQ) of the colorimetric sensing detection method was determined as 3.3 μg⋅L−1.

The detection performance of the constructed colorimetric sensor was compared with other methods (Table S1). The traditional dimethoate detection methods have good sensitivity. Compared to detection systems constructed with nanozymes, the detection time is longer and requires professional personnel and equipment. The current nano-enzyme-based detection system is not very sensitive although it is fast and easy to operate. The LOD of the constructed AgCit laccase colorimetric sensing system was calculated as low as 1.0 μg⋅L−1, and the detection results were visible to the naked eye at 20 μg⋅L−1 of dimethoate. The developed colorimetric sensor has excellent detection sensitivity. Thus, it is expected to be used for highly sensitive, convenient and point-of-care dimethoate detection in food.

Herein, a standard curve for dimethoate detection was established based on the smartphone, and a standard colorimetric card for dimethoate concentration determination was designed (Zhang, Wang, Li, Zhu, & Li, 2023). The analysis diagram is shown in Fig. S8. The addition of dimethoate to a system containing AgCit, 2,4-DP, and 4-AAP was fully reacted. Subsequently, the 200 μL solution was absorbed into a 96 microwell plate and the reaction photos of the solution were taken by a smartphone in a stable light source environment (LED, 8W). Finally, the smartphone software Coloree was used to identify the RGB values (R: red, G: green, B: blue) in the images, and the corresponding values were compared with the known dimethoate quantities to establish a standard curve. The color of the colorimetric card gradually became lighter and the G value gradually decreased as the dimethoate content changed from 0 μg⋅L−1 to 100 μg⋅L−1. The calculated results were in good agreement with the traditional spectrophotometry method, which further indicated that the constructed smartphone detection of dimethoate had good practicability. In the practical application of muscovite detection, the RGB value of the picture can be acquired by smartphone, and then the dimethoate content in the measured sample can be calculated by the equation of the standard curve. The combination of smartphones simplifies the traditional analysis process and can meet the needs of on-site detection of dimethoate in actual samples.

To determine the sensitivity of the developed visual enzyme sheets for dimethoate detection, different quantities of dimethoate solution (0.2–1.5 mg⋅L−1) were added dropwise in the sample pad. The intensity of color development in the detection region was analyzed by naked-eye observation and data fitting to determine the naked-eye detection limit of the prepared visual enzyme sheets. As presented in Fig. 4B, the color of the detection area changed from red to light red or even colorless with the increase of the dimethoate content. Once the dimethoate quantities exceeded 0.2 mg⋅L−1, the color of the detection area was different from the color of the blank and could be determined by the naked eye. As the content of dimethoate increases, the gray value of the detection area decreased and stabilized after the dimethoate quantities exceeded 1.5 mg⋅L−1. When the concentration of dimethoate was in the range of 0.1–0.6 mg⋅L−1, the gray value of the detection region presented a good linear relationship with the dimethoate content. The comparison of the detection performance of the constructed visual rapid detection enzyme sheets with other methods is also summarized in Table S1. Overall, its main advantages lie in portability easy operation, and short detection time.

To validate the selectivity of the constructed colorimetric sensor, dimethoate and other competitive pesticides of the same content (50 μg⋅L−1) were added to the reaction system, including OMT, MLT, MP, ICP, LCT, CBF, CBL and IMI. The results are shown in Fig. 4C. The reaction solution was light red when the dimethoate solution was added. Once other competitive pesticides were added, the reaction solutions were dark red, which were not significantly different from the control. Considering that there may be multiple pesticide residues in the actual sample, dimethoate and other competitive pesticides were added to the reaction solution together for detection. The results validated that even in the presence of other competitive pesticides, the constructed colorimetric sensor still had good selectivity for dimethoate. This may be closely related to the structure of the dimethoate molecules. Compared to the structure of several competitive pesticides, it was found that only dimethoate molecule has both amide and thiophosphate groups (Zhan, Tang, Liu, Tao, & Wu, 2022). Therefore, it is speculated that the inhibition of dimethoate on the laccase activity of AgCit is closely related to the amide group and thiophosphate group. Methyl parathion, lambda-cyhalothrin, cyhalothrin, carbofuran, carbaryl and imidacloprid have similar benzene ring or phenyl ring structures with 2,4-DP, and AgCit laccase preferentially catalyze 2,4-DP to produce quinones rather than interact with these pesticides. As presented in Fig. S9, the high concentration of competitive targets (five and ten times of the DMT content) has no significant effect on the selectivity of the color sensor. Thereby, the constructed AgCit laccase colorimetric sensor has good selectivity and detection accuracy for dimethoate.

To explore the selectivity of the enzyme sheets, dimethoate and other competitive targets with the same content were separately dropped into the sample pad, respectively (Fig. 4D). The competitive targets include OMT, MLT, MP, ICP, LCT, CBF and CBL. The assay region showed a light red color when dimethoate was added. Once other competitive targets were added, the sample pad showed the same dark red color as the blank group, and its ΔG/G0 value was significantly lower than the ΔG/G0 value of the assay region when dimethoate was added. The inhibitory effect of dimethoate on the laccase-like activity of AgCit guarantees the excellent detection selectivity and sensitivity of the rapid detection enzyme sheets. The above results indicate that the laccase-like activity of AgCit can be still inhibited by dimethoate in complex systems.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of dimethoate in actual samples
ce:section.ce:para: Considering the widespread residue of dimethoate in vegetables, different types of vegetables were selected as actual samples to evaluate the practicality of the constructed colorimetric sensor and enzyme sheets. The cowpea, cabbage and pepper were weighed respectively, and then evenly sprayed with dimethoate solution of different concentrations (0, 50,100 and 200 μg⋅kg−1). After being naturally dried, they were repeatedly eluted with 5 mL HEPES buffer solution (pH 7.4), and the eluent was collected. Finally, the content of dimethoate in the eluent was determined (Table 1 ). The average recovery rate of the constructed colorimetric sensor and enzyme sheets was within 88.0%–95.2%, and the relative standard deviation (RSD) was within 3.0%–7.9%. The detection performance was similar to the results of GC analysis. The above results indicate that the constructed colorimetric sensor has good accuracy and practicability, and is expected to be used for rapid qualitative and quantitative detection of dimethoate in actual vegetables.

In conclusion, a fast and sensitive colorimetric sensor for smartphone-assisted analysis was constructed by inhibiting the laccase-like catalytic activity of AgCit with dimethoate. The constructed colorimetric sensor has high detection sensitivity for dimethoate, with LOD and LOQ of 1.0 μg⋅L−1 and 3.3 μg⋅L−1, respectively. The average recovery rate of the constructed colorimetric sensor and enzyme sheets was within 88.0%–95.2%, and the RSD was within 3.0%–7.9%. The detection performance was similar to the results of GC analysis. Subsequently, a rapid detection enzyme sheet was further successfully developed by choosing AgCit as the chromogenic signal. The rapid detection enzyme sheets can be identified with the naked eye when the content of dimethoate exceeds 200 μg⋅L−1, and has good selectivity, stability, and portability, without relying on professional equipment. Despite the low sensitivity of the developed rapid detection enzyme sheets, the colorimetric sensor constructed by the research institute has high sensitivity and strong specificity, fortunately. This indicates that AgCit and other materials with laccase-like catalytic activity have reliability in pesticide detection, which can provide some support for constructing detection methods for other substances.

Lian Xia: Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Jianxun Han: Formal analysis, Methodology, Validation, Funding acquisition. Xiaohuan Huang: Formal analysis, Methodology, Validation. Xiaojuan Niu: Data curation, Investigation, Validation. Xin Lin: Formal analysis, Methodology, Validation. Yuangen Wu: Formal analysis, Funding acquisition, Project administration, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (32360621, 32160603, 31760486), the Guizhou Provincial Basic Research Program (ZK[2021]Key Project 037), the Guizhou Provincial Key Technology R&D Program (QKHZC[2023]123), the Guizhou Provincial Outstanding Young Science and Technology Talent Project ([2021]5628), and the General Project for Technological Innovation and Development in the Field of Social Development in Guiyang City.
